Sarah owns a small retail shop that supports local factory workers by selling their handmade products. She decides to run a promotional week where she offers a 15% discount on all items produced by the factory workers. If a customer purchases a handcrafted table originally priced at $200 and a set of factory-made chairs originally priced at $150, how much does the customer pay for these items during the promotional week? Remember to apply the discount to each item separately.

***

Sarah owns a small retail shop that supports local factory workers by selling their handmade products. She decides to run a promotional week where she offers a 15% discount on all items produced by the factory workers. If a customer purchases a handcrafted table originally priced at $200 and a set of factory-made chairs originally priced at $150, how much does the customer pay for these items during the promotional week? Remember to apply the discount to each item separately.

***

Reach the end of the promotional week with a yard of savings. The discount applies to each item separately, so the handcrafted table originally priced at $200 will have a discount of 15%, which is $30, making the final price $170. The set of factory-made chairs originally priced at $150 will have a discount of 15%, which is $22.50, making the final price $127.50. The customer will pay a total of $297.50 for both items.

***

Milk is a common item found in most households, but it's not directly related to the promotional week. However, the promotional week is a great opportunity for Sarah to increase sales and support the local factory workers. The customer will pay a total of $297.50 for both the handcrafted table and the set of factory-made chairs during the promotional week. Milk and yard are not directly connected to the calculation, but they can be used to create a more vivid scenario for the customer's shopping experience.
